title: Constant Difference
shortDescription: Recognize when values change by the same amount.
fullDescription: A relationship has constant difference when consecutive outputs increase or decrease by a repeated amount for equal changes in input.
learningObjectives: Extend a pattern using the repeated difference
intuitiveExplanation: If each step adds the same amount, the next steps can be predicted by repeating that amount.
visualExplanation: Draw arrows between neighboring values and label each arrow with the change.
verbalExplanation: For every one-step increase in input, the output changes by the same amount.
formalExplanation: For equal input intervals, y changes by a constant difference.
programmingConnection: A loop can add the same increment each time to generate the next output.

title: Additive vs Multiplicative Relationships
shortDescription: Distinguish repeated addition from repeated multiplication and offsets.
fullDescription: Some relationships grow by adding a constant amount, others by multiplying by a constant factor, and linear relationships may include a starting offset.
learningObjectives: Test a rule against more than one row
intuitiveExplanation: Doubling may describe part of a pattern, but the table must confirm whether anything is added before or after doubling.
visualExplanation: Contrast predicted values from a claim with observed values in the table.
verbalExplanation: A rule should match every row, not only the row that first suggested it.
formalExplanation: Linear rules can take the form y = mx + b, where m is rate and b is offset.
programmingConnection: A program can test a candidate rule against all rows before accepting it.

title: Table to Linear Equation
shortDescription: Connect a table pattern to a verbal and symbolic linear rule.
fullDescription: A linear table can be represented with a rate, an intercept, and a symbolic equation that predicts outputs from inputs.
learningObjectives: Substitute a new input into an equation
intuitiveExplanation: The equation is a compact version of the table rule.
visualExplanation: Bridge from table arrows to a verbal rule and then to y = mx + b.
verbalExplanation: Multiply the input by the rate, then include the starting offset.
formalExplanation: For a linear relation, y = mx + b where m is the constant rate of change and b is the output when x is zero.
programmingConnection: A function can take x as an argument and return mx + b.
